name: follow-up
description: Write follow-up emails that re-engage without being annoying. Uses proven psychology for follow-up sequences. 42% of replies come from follow-ups.
capabilities: Generate follow-up emails for any follow-up number (1, 2, or 3)
Apply three frameworks: The Bump, The Value-Add, and The Breakup
Adapt tone and length based on follow-up sequence position
Include new value propositions or angles without repeating content
Create permission-based closure emails that respect recipient time
author: humanizerai
securitySummary: Static analysis detected two potential issues that are both false positives. The 'blocker' finding incorrectly flagged line 3 as containing weak cryptography (it is only a description field). The 'external_commands' finding incorrectly flagged markdown code blocks at lines 76-82 as shell backtick execution (they are formatting delimiters showing email output format). This is a pure writing/documentation skill with no executable code, network access, or security risks.
valueStatement: Most sales opportunities die from silence, not rejection. This skill helps you write follow-up emails that re-engage prospects without being annoying using psychology-backed frameworks.
limitations: Cannot send emails directly - only generates text content
Requires manual input of original email context for best results
Does not track email open rates or response metrics
Templates follow general best practices but may need customization for specific industries

useCases.targetUser: Sales representatives and business development professionals
useCases.title: Sales Prospecting
useCases.description: Re-engage leads who did not respond to initial outreach with strategic follow-ups that add value instead of just checking in.

useCases.title: Job Application Follow-Up
useCases.description: Send professional follow-up emails after job interviews or application submissions to stay on hiring managers' radars.

useCases.title: Client Communication
useCases.description: Follow up on proposals, invoices, or pending decisions with clients while maintaining professional relationships.

promptTemplates.title: First Follow-Up
promptTemplates.prompt: Write a follow-up #1 email for this context: [paste original email]. It was sent 4 days ago. Add a new angle about [specific value proposition].
promptTemplates.scenario: Use when you have not received a response to your initial email within 3-5 days.

promptTemplates.title: Value-Add Follow-Up
promptTemplates.prompt: Write a follow-up #2 email that shares this insight: [paste article or insight]. Reference original discussion about [topic]. Keep it under 50 words.
promptTemplates.scenario: Use for your second follow-up when you want to provide additional value.

promptTemplates.title: Break-Up Email
promptTemplates.prompt: Write a follow-up #3 breakup email for [proportunity/project]. Give them an easy out and make it clear this is the last email.
promptTemplates.scenario: Use for your final follow-up after 14+ days to close the loop professionally.

promptTemplates.title: Quick Bump Follow-Up
promptTemplates.prompt: Write a short bump follow-up email. Original subject was [subject]. Just float it back to the top of their inbox with a friendly tone.
promptTemplates.scenario: Use when you want a light touch follow-up that assumes they are busy, not uninterested.

bestPractices: Always reference the original email or conversation briefly to provide context
Keep each follow-up shorter than the previous email - follow-ups should be scannable
Add new value, insights, or angles instead of repeating the same message
Space follow-ups appropriately: #1 after 3-5 days, #2 after 7-10 days, #3 after 14+ days
antiPatterns: Avoid apologetic language like 'I'm sure you're busy but...' - it sounds weak
Never send the exact same email twice without adding new context or value
Do not use guilt-trip phrases like 'I haven't heard back' or 'Why didn't you respond?'
Avoid passive-aggressive tone about lack of response - always assume positive intent

faq.question: What is the ideal time between follow-up emails?
faq.answer: Send follow-up #1 after 3-5 days, follow-up #2 after 7-10 days, and follow-up #3 after 14+ days. This gives prospects time to respond without being forgotten or overwhelmed.

faq.question: How long should follow-up emails be?
faq.answer: Keep follow-ups between 30-75 words. They should be shorter than your original email and easily scannable. Long follow-ups reduce response rates.

faq.question: What is the breakup email framework?
faq.answer: The breakup email is your third and final follow-up. It gives prospects an easy way to say they are not interested while creating urgency. Examples include 'Should I close your file?' or 'Is this project still on hold?'

faq.question: Should I change the subject line in follow-ups?
faq.answer: For follow-ups #1 and #2, use 'Re: [original subject]' to maintain thread context. For follow-up #3 (breakup email), consider a new subject line to capture attention.

faq.question: What if I do not have anything new to add in a follow-up?
faq.answer: Use The Bump framework for a short, friendly nudge that assumes they are busy. You can also share relevant industry news, a case study, or reframe your value proposition from a different angle.

faq.question: Can I use this skill for job application follow-ups?
faq.answer: Yes. The frameworks work well for job seekers. Adapt the language to be more formal and focus on your enthusiasm for the role rather than sales language.
